title: Lemonade scones
subtitle: Three-ingredient scones that come together in five minutes
excerpt: Self-raising flour, cream, and fizzy lemonade. The carbonation in the lemonade does the leavening; the cream does the fat and liquid in one. No rubbing in, no egg wash, no resting. Bake immediately for remarkably light, tender scones.
sourceNotes: Lemonade scones are a British and Australian home-baking tradition that gained wide circulation in the twentieth century. The use of carbonated lemonade as a leavening agent alongside cream is documented in Australian Women's Weekly baking columns and in British women's magazine recipe collections of the 1960s and 1970s. The recipe is fully public domain; no single source claims authorship.

content.text: The logic of lemonade scones is simple.

provides structure and some leavening. Double cream provides the fat and most of the liquid. Fizzy lemonade provides the remaining liquid, extra sweetness, and, through its carbonation, a boost of lift as the bubbles expand in the heat of the oven. The result is a scone that comes together in minutes with almost no technique required.

The main rules are speed and restraint: use cold cream, use the lemonade straight from the fridge, and do not overmix. Once the liquid goes into the flour, mix only enough to bring the dough together, pat it out, and get it in the oven quickly before the carbonation escapes.

Heat the oven to 210°C fan (230°C conventional) and line a baking tray with baking paper. Put {{self-raising-flour}} in a large bowl. Pour in {{double-cream}} and {{lemonade}} and mix briefly with a fork until the dough just comes together. It will be soft and slightly sticky. Do not knead or overwork it. Turn out onto a well-floured surface and pat to 3 cm thickness; do not use a rolling pin. Cut into rounds with a floured straight-sided cutter or slice into squares with a sharp knife. Place on the lined tray and bake for 12 to 15 minutes until risen and golden on top, with sides that spring back when pressed. Transfer to a cooling rack and eat as soon as they are cool enough to handle.
The lemonade must be full-sugar; diet versions contain less carbon dioxide and produce a flatter result.
Add the lemonade to the bowl last and mix immediately. Letting the lemonade sit in the bowl before mixing loses carbonation and reduces the rise.
These scones are sweeter than a standard recipe. For a less sweet version, replace up to half the lemonade with full-fat milk and add a pinch of salt.
